commands.review_summary:
The PR escapes Teams mention display names before inserting them into `<at>...</at>` tags and adds parser regression tests for markup and closing-tag injection names.

PR surface: Source +4, Tests +29. Total +33 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. from source inspection: current main builds `<at>${trimmedName}</at>` directly, and `buildActivity` sends that parser output as Teams activity text/entities. I did not establish a live Teams reproduction in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] No automated code repair is needed; the branch needs contributor or maintainer real Teams proof, or explicit maintainer acceptance of parser-only proof.

target.title: fix(msteams): escape markup in mention display names
target.body: **Summary:** Teams mention display names containing `&`/`<`/`>` were inserted into `` markup verbatim, corrupting the adaptive-card markup (a crafted name could even break out of the mention tag). This escapes those metacharacters so the rendered mention markup is always well-formed, while the readable `mentioned.name` is unchanged. ## What Problem This Solves `parseMentions` in `extensions/msteams/src/mentions.ts` rewrites `@[Name](id)` syntax into Teams `Name` mention tags and a matching `entities[]` array. The display name was inserted into the `...` tag **verbatim**. When a Teams user/bot display name contains markup metacharacters (`&`, `<`, `>`) — which are legal in Teams display names — the raw characters leak straight into the adaptive-card markup. For an input like: ```text Hello @[Tom & Jerry](28:abc-def-ghi)! ``` the rendered tag became `Tom & Jerry`, where the embedded `...` is interpreted as live markup and the bare `&` is an invalid entity. A crafted name such as `AB` could even break out of the mention tag entirely. ## Fix Escape the markup metacharacters of the mention display name before embedding it inside the `` tag, while leaving the structured `mentioned.name` field as the original (un-escaped) human-readable name. A small `escapeTeamsMentionText` helper replaces `&` → `&`, `<` → `<`, `>` → `>` (ampersand first, so it is not double-escaped). The text inside `...` and the `entity.text` are now always well-formed markup. ## Evidence Ran the **real** `parseMentions` function from the PR branch in an isolated worktree (`node --import tsx`), feeding it a name with `&`/`<`/`>` and a valid Bot Framework id (`28:...`) so the mention is actually recognized and rendered. `BEFORE` reproduces the pre-fix verbatim insertion; `AFTER` calls the shipped fix and asserts no raw metacharacters survive inside the tag. ```text $ node --import tsx demo.mts === INPUT === "Hello @[Tom & Jerry](28:abc-def-ghi)!" === BEFORE (verbatim, broken markup) === rendered tag : Tom & Jerry contains raw '&' : true contains raw '': true contains raw '': true === AFTER (real parseMentions output) === formatted text : Hello Tom & <b>Jerry</b>! entity text : Tom & <b>Jerry</b> entity name : "Tom & Jerry" === AFTER assertions === escaped '&' -> & : true escaped '<' -> < : true escaped '>' -> > : true inner has raw '&' (unescaped): false inner has raw '<' : false inner has raw '>' : false markup-safe (no raw metachars): true ``` `BEFORE` leaks `&`, ``, and `` into the markup. `AFTER` (the real fix) emits `&`/`<`/`>` in both `formatted text` and `entity text`, with **zero** raw metacharacters left inside the `` tag, while `mentioned.name` still holds the original readable `Tom & Jerry`. ## Tests `extensions/msteams/src/mentions.test.ts` adds vitest coverage that is red before the fix and green after: - `escapes markup characters in mention tags and keeps entity text aligned` — asserts `parseMentions("Hi @[Tom & Jerry](28:abc-123)")` produces `Tom & <b>Jerry</b>`, that `entity.text` matches the formatted tag, and that `mentioned.name` keeps the un-escaped name. - `escapes closing at tags inside mention names` — asserts a name of `AB` is escaped to `A</at><at>B` so it cannot break out of the mention tag (`result.text` no longer contains the raw `AB`). Existing tests (plain names, multiple mentions, non-ASCII names) continue to pass, confirming no regression for names without metacharacters.
